mnemonics.title: CMON Key Resources
mnemonics.content: Remember the 4 key resources using CMON: CPU, Memory, Operations (I/O), Network - these are what you monitor for failure prediction

mnemonics.title: PARE Forecasting Steps
mnemonics.content: PARE for capacity: Prepare data, Analyze trends, Review SLA requirements, Estimate future needs

mnemonics.title: ALERT Alert Threshold Levels
mnemonics.content: Set ALERT levels: Alert (70-80%), Warning (80-90%), Critical (90%+) with clear action plans for each

mnemonics.title: SCALE Scaling Types
mnemonics.content: SCALE: Scale Up (vertical - more resources), Scale Out (horizontal - more machines)

acronyms.usage: The main processor that executes instructions; high utilization indicates need for capacity planning
Short-term memory for running applications; exhaustion causes system slowdowns or crashes
Contract defining performance and availability standards that capacity planning must meet
Measures storage performance; critical metric for storage capacity planning
Metrics used to measure system performance and guide capacity decisions
Used in automated scaling to trigger capacity adjustments programmatically

visualTips.visualization: Think of a car's fuel gauge - when it approaches 'E', you know you need fuel soon. Similarly, when CPU/memory gauge approaches 100%, you need to add resources
Picture a thermometer with color zones: Green (0-70%, safe), Yellow (70-80%, warning), Orange (80-90%, critical), Red (90%+, emergency)
Think of a slope on a hill - if the trend line points upward toward 100%, you're heading toward failure. The steeper the slope, the sooner you'll hit capacity
Imagine a glass being filled - headroom is the empty space at the top that prevents overflow. More headroom = more safety margin

analogies.analogy: Like a city planner anticipating population growth - must ensure roads, schools, and utilities can handle future residents before problems occur
Like a highway during rush hour - when too many cars (requests) try to use limited lanes (resources), traffic (system) slows to a crawl or stops
Like a smoke detector - it warns you of danger before a fire (system failure) destroys everything, giving time to act
Like a restaurant hiring more staff during lunch rush and sending them home when traffic dies down - matching resources to demand
